narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 57% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions to ensure strong outcomes for our students. As a male Asian student from a lower-middle income family planning to attend full-time with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical student's—you have a solid foundation in some areas, but certain aspects of your profile raised concerns for the model. Your position in the bottom decile for school grades in both Year 1 and Year 3 stood out as the most influential factor, strongly suggesting a higher risk of bar failure compared to peers who typically rank around the middle of their classes. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 19.3, which is notably lower than the average of 36.7 and further pushed the prediction toward failure. Your Year 1 decile rank, also at the bottom, ranked third and reinforced that early academic struggles could carry over. Your family's lower-middle income level came fourth, as it sometimes correlates with additional barriers not seen in higher-income averages. Being Asian, which is less common in the dataset than White students at 83.8%, played a moderate role in the same direction, followed by your near-average undergraduate GPA. Your gender as male and full-time status had the least sway but still nudged slightly toward the riskier outcome. Overall, these elements combined to tip the scales against you when compared to typical applicants who pass the bar more reliably with stronger early grades, higher LSATs, and middle-of-the-pack rankings. The good news is you can take action on changeable factors like retaking the LSAT with targeted prep to boost your score toward the average, which could dramatically shift future predictions. Building study habits now might also help improve class rankings in any further schooling, and seeking scholarships or support programs for lower-income students could address income-related hurdles. With focused effort on these, you're well-positioned to strengthen your profile for another shot at admission down the line. Keep pushing—you've got the determination it takes.
